stories.title: Essex Capital Markets Arranges $20M for River North Office Conversion
stories.excerpt: Essex Capital Markets completed the arrangement of a $19.57 million acquisition and construction loan for the redevelopment of 226 W. Ontario Street in Chicago’s River North neighborhood. The financing will support the conversion of the existing office building into a 70-unit residential property, including 60 apartments within the existing structure and 10 additional units through a planned two-story vertical expansion. On behalf of the sponsor, Essex Capital Markets secured financing through a local banking partner, delivering a two-phase funding structure designed to support the project’s acquisition and construction timeline. The financing includes an 80% loan-to-cost ratio and full-term interest-only payments. The transaction was facilitated by Directors Quinn Keenan and Asher Motew. “This was not a standard construction loan,” said Motew. “The lender had to get comfortable with the conversion of an existing 113-year-old building, the planned vertical addition, and a funding schedule that matched the sponsor’s construction timeline. We found a local bank that understood the project and structured the facility so the sponsor could stage its equity contribution without giving up proceeds.” The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post Essex Capital Markets Arranges $20M for River North Office Conversion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Essex arranged $19.57M acquisition/construction loan for 226 W. Ontario, River North Chicago
70-unit conversion of 113-year-old office building; 80% LTC, interest-only structure
Local bank financing enabled staged equity contribution; vertical expansion adds 10 units
stories.summary: Essex Capital Markets secured a $19.57 million two-phase loan for River North office-to-residential conversion at 226 W. Ontario in Chicago, delivering 70 apartments (60 in existing structure, 10 via two-story expansion). The 80% LTC deal with interest-only payments required bespoke underwriting for a 113-year-old building and phased funding aligned to sponsor's construction schedule. Local banking partner structured the facility to allow staged equity without forcing early proceeds distribution.
stories.impact: Chicago's office conversion pipeline remains viable where local lenders understand conversion risk; confirms River North rental demand supports redevelopment feasibility at mid-range unit counts. Lack of national CMBS/agency appetite for conversion projects makes local relationships critical to closing deals in tight credit environments.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group partners Shinsegae Group (South Korea) for $500M luxury fund
Initial Seoul mixed-use project (Aman hotel, residences, club); expansion into Asia, Middle East, US
Aman brand sold $9B in branded residences in four years; 20 hotels under construction globally
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group have formed a $500 million luxury development fund targeting hotels and branded residences across Asia, the Middle East, and the U.S., launching with a mixed-use project in Seoul's Gangnam District. Aman has generated $9 billion in branded residence sales over four years and operates approximately 20 hotels under construction worldwide, including Aman Beverly Hills (which has secured $1 billion+ in pre-sales). The fund structure pairs Shinsegae's retail expertise with OKO's development and hospitality capabilities.
stories.impact: Limited direct bearing on U.S. multifamily development; primarily signals luxury branded-residences and hotel capital redeploying to Asia. However, Aman's $9B residential sales rate and aggressive development pace (20 properties under construction) illustrates how ultra-luxury and hospitality-anchored projects can compete for institutional capital, potentially tightening availability of sources for traditional mid-market apartment financing.

stories.title: Amazon to Begin Work on $98M Terrell Distribution Center
stories.excerpt: Amazon is constructing a new $98 million, 1.2 million-square-foot regional distribution center in Terrell, Texas. The Dallas Morning News reports construction could begin as early as August 2026, with completion slated for next year. Amazon bought the 120-acre development site last year from Hunt Realty Investments. Located at 3201 Airport Road, the property is between the U.S. Route 80 and Interstate 20 corridors. Amazon is also adding to its metro footprint by adding another 1.7 million-square-foot distribution center project. Construction kicked off two years ago in Cleburne, Texas, with the building on track for delivery this quarter. Since 2010, Amazon has invested more than $100 billion across Texas, creating 100 fulfillment, sortation and delivery centers. The post Amazon to Begin Work on $98M Terrell Distribution Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Amazon commences $98M, 1.2M-SF regional distribution center in Terrell, Texas; start Aug 2026
120-acre site between US-80 and I-20; delivery 2027. Adds to 1.7M-SF Cleburne facility (completion Q1 2026)
Amazon invested $100B+ in Texas since 2010; 100+ fulfillment/sortation/delivery centers statewide
stories.summary: Amazon is constructing a $98 million, 1.2 million-square-foot regional distribution center in Terrell, Texas (southeast of Dallas), with construction starting August 2026 and completion in 2027. The company is simultaneously completing a 1.7 million-square-foot facility in Cleburne this quarter. Since 2010, Amazon has invested over $100 billion across Texas, operating 100+ fulfillment, sortation, and delivery centers.
stories.impact: Continued Amazon logistics consolidation in Dallas–Fort Worth metro drives sustained employment growth, wage support, and population in-migration to secondary markets (Terrell, Cleburne). This reinforces rental demand expansion eastward and southward from Dallas proper; developers should monitor absorption and rent trajectory in DFW suburban/exurban multifamily inventory, particularly Class B/C stock near I-20 and emerging job centers.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties formed $1.9B nontraded REIT consolidating 45 properties, 5,600+ units
8 Carlisle (Manhattan Financial District, 462 units) secured $377M construction loan; 64-story, 789-ft tower
Grubb drew $617M total financing (incl. $240M credit facility) to enable larger balance sheet and borrowing capacity
stories.summary: Grubb Properties consolidated its portfolio into Link Apartments REIT, a $1.9 billion nontraded vehicle comprising 45 properties and 5,600+ units. The restructuring funded a $377 million construction loan for 8 Carlisle (462-unit rental tower in Lower Manhattan's Financial District), combining a $300 million senior loan (Maxim Capital) and $77 million mezzanine (Skylight Real Estate), plus a separate $240 million credit facility. Grubb secured $617 million across related transactions to scale balance-sheet financing capacity.
stories.impact: REIT formation signals Grubb's strategic pivot toward institutional-grade capital access and leverage; demonstrates developer sophistication in using vehicle restructuring to unlock larger construction loans and revolving facilities. The 8 Carlisle financing (strong construction execution in constrained Lower Manhattan) validates sponsor credibility; watch for Grubb to acquire/develop additional trophy assets in high-barrier coastal markets using recapitalized balance sheet.

stories.title: CDK Lending Supplies $37M Loan for Newark Apartments Project
stories.excerpt: RBH Group has landed $36.7 million of pre-development financing for a mixed-use multifamily project in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. CDK Lending provided the loan on the developer’s planned 420-unit apartment complex with ground-floor retail space at 101 Market Street in Newark’s Four Corners neighborhood near downtown. The debt pays off a previous loan provided by Goldman Sachs for the long-planned development, which is located within a 5.65-acre land assemblage site. Meridian Capital Group arranged the transaction with a team led by David Bollag and Naphtali Marrus. “Newark’s downtown has seen remarkable momentum over the past decade, and a development-ready assemblage of this scale in the central business district is exceptionally hard to come by,” Bollag said in a statement. RBH Group has been assembling land for a mixed-use development consisting of 49 separate contiguous lots since 2005, according to Bollag. The firm also plans to commence a 241-unit multifamily project at nearby 193 Market Street next year and is eyeing other developments at the site roughly half a mile from Newark Penn Station. Newark-based RBH Group is also developing the Teachers Village project at 243 Halsey Street in Newark featuring 203 apartments geared toward local educators. CDK Lending and RBH Group did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: RBH Group secured $36.7M pre-development financing from CDK Lending for Newark mixed-use project
101 Market Street (Four Corners): 420 apartments, ground-floor retail; replaces Goldman Sachs bridge loan
5.65-acre land assemblage (49 lots) assembled since 2005; separate 241-unit project also planned
stories.summary: RBH Group obtained $36.7 million in pre-development financing from CDK Lending (arranged by Meridian Capital Group) for a 420-unit mixed-use development at 101 Market Street in Newark's Four Corners district. The loan refinances prior Goldman Sachs bridge debt; the 5.65-acre assemblage (49 contiguous lots) has been under consolidation since 2005. RBH also plans a separate 241-unit multifamily project at the site.
stories.impact: Newark downtown momentum (per Meridian's statement) confirms sustained institutional appetite for infill multifamily development in legacy industrial Northeast metros; long land-assemblage timeline (18+ years) underscores regulatory/entitlement friction but CDK Lending's commitment validates market fundamentals. Developers targeting older Northeast industrial cores (Jersey City, Hoboken, Newark) should monitor CDK and regional bridge lenders for pre-dev and construction financing; Goldman's retreat signals institutional capital's renewed (if cautious) interest in mid-tier Northeast urban projects.

stories.title: Insurance Giant Fidelity Takes 20K SF at SL Green’s 711 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: SL Green Realty has signed two deals totaling 34,564 square feet at its 20-story Midtown East office building at 711 Third Avenue, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest deal, title insurance and settlement provider Fidelity National Title Insurance signed a new lease for 19,966 square feet on a portion of the seventh floor for a period of 10 years, according to the landlord. Fidelity’s website lists its current address as 485 Lexington Avenue, a five-minute walk from its new location. It is unclear whether the firm’s new spot on Third Avenue is a relocation of its current offices or a new location. CBRE’s Joseph Fabrizi and Arkady Smolyansky represented the tenant in the deal. Fabrizi did not immediately respond to CO’s request for comment. SL Green did not disclose the asking rent for the lease, but the average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers data. Meanwhile, SL Green also signed a 14,598-square-foot renewal lease with Ackman Ziff Real Estate Group for a period of eight and a half years. The real estate capital advisory firm is located on a portion of the building’s 11th floor. Ackman Ziff moved its headquarters to 711 Third Avenue in 2015, signing a 10-year, 19,025-square-foot lease, CO reported at the time. It is unclear when the firm downsized from its initial space. Ackman represented itself in the lease renewal. The firm did not immediately respond to a request for comment. “We’re delighted that each of these tenants has selected to locate their office at 711 Third Avenue,” Steven Durels, executive vice president and director of leasing and real property at SL Green, said in a statement. “Leasing velocity in Midtown Manhattan continues to strengthen as tenants seek well located buildings with strong sponsorship.” Built in 1955, 711 Third Avenue is situated between East 44th and East 45th streets, one block east of Grand Central Terminal
bullets: Fidelity National Title Insurance leased 19,966 SF at SL Green's 711 Third Avenue, Midtown East; 10-year term
Midtown office average asking rent $84.99/SF (Q2 2026); Fidelity relocating from 485 Lexington (5-minute walk)
SL Green also renewed 14,598 SF with Ackman Ziff Real Estate Group; office market stabilizing
stories.summary: SL Green Realty signed a 10-year lease with Fidelity National Title Insurance for 19,966 square feet on the seventh floor of 711 Third Avenue in Midtown East; Fidelity is relocating from 485 Lexington Avenue, a short walk away. SL Green also renewed a 14,598-square-foot lease with Ackman Ziff Real Estate Group. Midtown office space averaged $84.99 per square foot in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact; signals Midtown East office market stabilization and modest intra-market tenant shuffling, not flight. However, sustained office leasing in Midtown (with rents holding near $85/SF) suggests limited near-term office-to-residential conversion pipeline in the core; conversion economics remain challenging at current office basis and rents. Developers should prioritize secondary Manhattan submarkets (Astoria, Long Island City) and outer boroughs for conversion optionality.

stories.title: Mesa Entertainment Venue Reveals Open Date
stories.excerpt: The Nox, a new, large-scale live music and entertainment venue, is under construction in southeast Mesa, Arizona. It’s set to open in late 2027. It is slated to be a year-round entertainment destination for the Southeast Valley, hosting concerts, comedy, festivals, dinner theater, and corporate events. The 70,000-square-foot venue is expected to look like a “Chicago warehouse,” with brick, iron, and arches, and a focus on acoustics. It will be near Ray and Power roads. It will feature a modular concert hall, recording studio, and flexible event space for concerts, comedy, aiming to serve the growing East Valley and reduce the need for residents to travel for entertainment. Developed by the Bongiorno family and Streamline Cos., it will have a capacity of up to 2,000 people. Besides regular attendees, the building will include a mezzanine-level VIP lounge with a private bar and seating as well as numerous food and beverage options. The post Mesa Entertainment Venue Reveals Open Date appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nox live-music venue (Bongiorno/Streamline Cos.) opening late 2027 in southeast Mesa, Arizona
70,000-SF, 2,000-capacity facility near Ray & Power roads; year-round concerts, comedy, festivals, dinner theater
Designed to serve growing East Valley and reduce resident travel for entertainment
stories.summary: The Nox, a 70,000-square-foot live-music and entertainment venue developed by the Bongiorno family and Streamline Cos., is slated to open in late 2027 in southeast Mesa, Arizona near Ray and Power roads. With a 2,000-person capacity and programming for concerts, comedy, festivals, and dinner theater, it aims to serve the growing Southeast Valley and reduce resident dependence on downtown Phoenix entertainment travel.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; entertainment venue development in exurban East Valley (Mesa, Tempe, Chandler) supports quality-of-life amenities that enhance rental appeal and help justify density in suburban/exurban markets. However, this is supplementary to core demand drivers (employment, population, cost). Developers should note Mesa's incremental progress in placemaking; monitor broader East Valley multifamily demand as tech/logistics employment migration (Amazon, Intel) materializes.

stories.title: Centaur Sells Panther National Golf Resort and a Housing Dev for $191M
stories.excerpt: Ohana Real Estate Investors paid $191.2 million for the Panther National golf resort and a luxury housing development in Palm Beach Gardens, Fla., property records show. The asset spans 392 acres within the Avenir master development at 10391 Panther National Boulevard. Panther National includes an 18-hole championship golf course designed by one of the sport’s legends, Jack Nicklaus, and two-time PGA major champion Justin Thomas, as well as a par-3 course and 68 single-family homes. Developers also plan to add another 150 single-family homes. Swiss developer Centaur Holdings sold the property. J.P. Morgan Chase supplied a $115 million acquisition loan to the Austin, Texas-based, hospitality-focused firm. The deal ranks among South Florida’s largest golf course sales in recent years, though many buyers have ultimately redeveloped their courses into residential communities. In 2021, Centaur Holdings purchased the land for $60 million from the master developer, Coral Gables-based Landstar. Two years later, it completed the 18-hole golf course. In 2024, Centaur secured a $160 million debt package from Southern Realty Trust and Sunrise Realty Trust, of which $85 million went toward refinancing an existing note and the remaining $75 million went toward building out 180 homes and a 60,000-square-foot clubhouse. The debt has been repaid, the lender announced. The purchase comes a year after Brookfield Properties sold the nearby 800-acre PGA National Resort for $425 million. The property includes 360 hotel rooms, a 40,000-square-foot spa, and 99 golf holes. Representatives for Centaur Holdings and Ohana Real Estate Investors did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Ohana Real Estate Investors bought Panther National golf resort + 68 existing homes for $191.2M in Palm Beach Gardens
392-acre Avenir master-plan site; plans 150 additional single-family homes. J.P. Morgan supplied $115M acquisition loan
Centaur (Swiss developer) acquired asset 2021 for $60M; completed 18-hole Nicklaus/Thomas-designed course
stories.summary: Ohana Real Estate Investors purchased the Panther National golf resort and 68 completed single-family homes within the Avenir master development in Palm Beach Gardens, Florida for $191.2 million; J.P. Morgan Chase provided a $115 million acquisition loan. The 392-acre asset includes an 18-hole championship golf course (designed by Jack Nicklaus and Justin Thomas), a par-3 course, and plans for 150 additional single-family homes. Centaur Holdings (Swiss developer) sold the property after purchasing it in 2021 for $60 million.
stories.impact: Signals continued golf-course-to-residential conversion trend in South Florida; rising land values and limited golf demand favor density. Developers should monitor Avenir and similar master-plan communities for multifamily densification opportunities adjacent to or integrated into single-family/resort clusters. However, Ohana's focus on single-family expansion (150 new homes) suggests market segmentation—multifamily plays here would target adjacent infill or ancillary resort/hospitality properties, not the golf preserve itself.

stories.title: Endeavor Building Nearly 200K-SF Austin Offices, to Include HQ
stories.excerpt: Endeavor Real Estate Group has closed on financing and is soon to begin work on a new 198,000-square-foot office and retail building on the western edge of downtown Austin. After construction, Endeavor will call the building its new headquarters. Dubbed 5th & Walsh, the project has been in the works since 2022. Located at 1200 W. 5th St., it will stand five stories atop 1.4 acres. Of the project’s 198,000 total square feet, 21,000 will be ground-floor restaurant and retail space, and the upper four floors will be office space. Endeavor will occupy 25,000 square feet when it makes 5th & Walsh its new headquarters. Endeavor’s current headquarters is at downtown Austin’s 500 W. 5th St. Besides it office needs, Endeavor says 85,000 square feet of office space is already leased by undisclosed tenants. Endeavor representatives Fernando Urrutia, Anne Swift and Colton McCasland will be handling the marketing and leasing of the office space, while the retail spaces will be overseen by Billy Osherow and Evan Deitch. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Endeavor Building Nearly 200K-SF Austin Offices, to Include HQ appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate Group closed financing, beginning construction on 5th & Walsh office headquarters in downtown Austin
198,000-SF five-story building (1.4 acres); 21,000 SF ground-floor retail, 177,000 SF office (85,000 already leased)
Endeavor occupies 25,000 SF; HQ relocation from 500 W. 5th St.; retail/office leasing underway
stories.summary: Endeavor Real Estate Group closed financing and is commencing construction on 5th & Walsh, a 198,000-square-foot five-story office and retail building at 1200 W. 5th Street on downtown Austin's western edge. The project includes 21,000 square feet of ground-floor restaurant/retail and 177,000 square feet of office space, with 85,000 SF already leased by undisclosed tenants and Endeavor occupying 25,000 SF as its new headquarters. Endeavor is relocating from 500 W. 5th Street.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact; signals local real estate operator confidence in downtown Austin office fundamentals (Endeavor's own HQ commitment validates market). However, continued downtown office development in Austin—particularly in Class A new construction—may compete for young-professional and corporate-relocation talent, potentially increasing multifamily rental demand in nearby South Congress, Rainey, and adjacent mixed-use corridors. Developers should monitor office and multifamily absorption in downtown Austin submarkets to assess demand elasticity.

stories.title: NYC Landlords Sue Rent Guidelines Board Over Rent Freeze
stories.excerpt: Following the city’s rent freeze decision in June, it was very much expected that angry New York City landlords — now burdened with even more maintenance costs and increased upkeep on rent-stabilized apartments — would take legal action to overturn the vote. And now it’s a reality. A group of five small landlords who own rent-stabilized apartments in the city filed a lawsuit Wednesday in a New York state court against the city’s Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) over its 7-1 vote last month to freeze rents for both one- and two-year leases on rent-stabilized apartments in the city. The landlords — using the LLC Kenilworth Holdings — claim in the suit that the RGB went through a “sham process” before the vote, and that Mayor Zohran Mamdani influenced the outcome of the decision. The suit also claims that the mayor appointed RGB members “predisposed to delivering a freeze,” and that the RGB “ignored evidence of soaring landlord costs and manipulated the data to understate the impact of these costs.” Additionally, the landlords wrote in the suit that the RGB disregarded petitioners who showed that “they and similarly situated landlords cannot survive a rent freeze.” “The RGB failed to meet its legal mandate of setting rent adjustments based on its own data and research, which strongly justified a rent increase,” said Ann Korchak, board president of Small Property Owners of New York (SPONY), whose members are plaintiffs in the lawsuit. “The rent freeze was illegal. The process was illegal and stacked against small owners of rent-stabilized properties. We are hopeful the court will address this injustice.” It’s the first time the city’s real estate industry has challenged the legal basis for the rent freeze, and the first time it has legally challenged Mamdani since he took office. “Zohran Mamdani promised to deliver a rent freeze during his campaign and then he went to extraordinary lengths as a new mayor to guarantee it,” Randy Mastro, a lawyer for th
bullets: NYC landlord group (Kenilworth Holdings LLC) sued Rent Guidelines Board over June 2026 rent freeze (7-1 vote)
Plaintiffs claim RGB followed 'sham process,' Mayor Zohran Mamdani influenced vote, data manipulation alleged
Rent freeze applies to both one- and two-year rent-stabilized leases citywide
stories.summary: A coalition of five small NYC landlords (operating under Kenilworth Holdings LLC) filed suit in state court challenging the Rent Guidelines Board's June 2026 7-1 vote to freeze rents on both one- and two-year rent-stabilized leases. The suit alleges the RGB conducted a "sham process," that Mayor Zohran Mamdani improperly influenced the outcome by appointing predisposed board members, and that the RGB ignored landlord cost evidence and manipulated data to understate operational impacts.
stories.impact: Rent freeze in NYC multifamily legally constrains landlord revenue and refinancing capacity on stabilized portfolios; litigation risk adds uncertainty to stabilized-asset valuations and refinancing timelines. Developers should avoid or heavily scrutinize acquisition of rent-stabilized buildings; market bifurcation deepens (market-rate new construction remains attractive; legacy stabilized stock increasingly challenged). Expect litigation to extend 12–24 months; RGB policy volatility will suppress institutional appetite for stabilized-heavy platforms, driving capital concentration in market-rate new construction and value-add conversions.

stories.title: National Property Planning 1.1M-SF Katy Industrial Park
stories.excerpt: National Property Holdings plans to develop Katy Prairie Business Park, a 1,083,420-square-foot industrial project in Katy, Texas. CBRE’s Faron Wiley, Billy Gold, Charles Herold and Jett Bush have been selected to market the project for lease.   The Class A project will include two buildings: Phase I, totaling 402,480 square feet, and Phase II, totaling 680,940 square feet. The company developed a Domino’s warehouse across the street (photo) A key differentiator for Katy Prairie Business Park is its favorable tax environment, including potential tax abatement opportunities in Waller County and competitive property tax rates that may help reduce long-term operating costs.  Located at 500 and 700 Igloo Road, Katy Prairie Business Park has visibility along the Katy Freeway, one of Houston’s most heavily traveled corridors, offering exceptional exposure and access. Nearby users include major consumer goods companies, large-scale manufacturing operations, solar energy and emerging technology companies. The post National Property Planning 1.1M-SF Katy Industrial Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: National Property Holdings planning Katy Prairie Business Park: 1.08M-SF industrial, two phases, Waller County, Katy, Texas
Phase I: 402,480 SF; Phase II: 680,940 SF. Located 500/700 Igloo Road (visibility on Katy Freeway, I-20 corridor)
Tax abatement opportunities; Class A spec; CBRE marketing; proximity to consumer goods, manufacturing, tech users
stories.summary: National Property Holdings is developing Katy Prairie Business Park, a 1.08 million-square-foot Class A industrial project in two phases (Phase I: 402,480 SF; Phase II: 680,940 SF) at 500 and 700 Igloo Road in Katy, Texas. The site offers visibility along the Katy Freeway and proximity to I-20, with favorable tax environment including potential Waller County abatements. Nearby tenants include major consumer goods, manufacturing, solar energy, and emerging technology companies; CBRE is marketing the project.
stories.impact: Industrial logistics expansion in Katy/greater Houston underscores continued supply-chain consolidation westward; Amazon, e-commerce, and tech manufacturing growth support sustained job/wage growth in outer Houston suburbs. Multifamily developers should monitor logistics employment corridors along I-20 west of Houston (Katy, Waller County) for rent growth and new-unit demand; secondary and tertiary markets (Tomball, Hockley) will capture workforce housing demand. Track Waller County entitlements and tax policy; industrial growth accelerates residential demand in underserved suburban nodes.

stories.title: New York City Investment Sales Exceeding 2025 Pace: Report
stories.excerpt: New York City investment sales dipped 10 percent over the second quarter of 2026, according to Avison Young’s latest property sales report. Under the hood, however, the city’s commercial real estate market is barreling past its 2025 pace. Overall investment sales in New York City rose annually by 60 percent in the first half of 2026 as capital markets and office financing reopened, according to the report, with transaction counts and dollar volumes rising annually across Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens and the Bronx. The report did not track Staten Island. The geopolitical and economic upheaval that defined early 2026 did not go unfelt, however, as quarterly sales volume declined to $5.42 billion citywide from $5.68 billion. Above the quarterly noise was a market in a welcome recovery cycle. The city is on pace for $22.87 billion in annual sales in 2026, according to the report, approaching New York City’s 10-year average of $23.4 billion. Brandon Polakoff, a principal and head of New York City investment sales at Avison Young, compared the city’s path of recovery since 2023 to the years following the Global Financial Crisis, which culminated in a 2015 peak for New York City investment sales. “Year by year, we’re working in the right direction,” Polakoff said. Manhattan counted 94 sales in the second quarter of 2026, led in volume by Extell Development’s $451 million move on 405 Park Avenue in May. Other defining deals included Sovereign Partners’ $378 million purchase of 575 Fifth Avenue and the $280 million sale of 250 West 57th Street to Namdar Realty Group. Development was Manhattan’s breakout asset class for the second quarter, according to Polakoff, climbing year-over-year from three to 13 sales totaling $707 million. “We found the demand for development to be astronomical in the past six to eight months,” Polakoff said, pointing to the difficulty of acquiring existing multifamily and the comparative feasibility of stringing together 99-unit rentals to avoid
bullets: NYC investment sales up 60% YoY (H1 2026) vs. Q2 2026 down 10% QoQ to $5.42B from $5.68B
2026 annual pace: $22.87B (vs. 10-year average $23.4B); capital markets and office financing reopened
Transaction counts and dollar volumes rising across Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx
stories.summary: New York City's investment sales surged 60 percent year-over-year in the first half of 2026 despite a 10 percent sequential decline in Q2 (to $5.42 billion from $5.68 billion). The city is on track for $22.87 billion in annual sales in 2026, approaching the 10-year average of $23.4 billion. Transaction counts and dollar volumes rose across all five boroughs (excluding Staten Island) as capital markets and office financing reopened following early-2026 geopolitical and economic uncertainty.
stories.impact: NYC commercial real estate capital markets recovering faster than expected; multifamily investor demand rebounding (reflected in transaction count/volume increases across boroughs). Developers should capitalize on re-opening financing window and investor appetite; expect cap rate compression and increased acquisition competition for quality assets. Refinancing windows tightening for older debt; bridge and construction financing availability improving. Monitor multifamily cap-rate trends in Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens; repositioning and renovation opportunities emerging as investors increase activity.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC permits week of 7/17/26: 112,279-SF mixed-use (50 apts, Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts); 82,731-SF mixed-use (86 apts, Queens)
Bronx: two nine-story apartment buildings (99 units each, 128,738 SF combined, Tremont/East Tremont Avenue)
Major alterations: Brookfield 250 Vestry (192,245 SF, Financial District); JPMorgan Chase 126 Myrtle Ave, Brooklyn (30,000 SF)
stories.summary: NYC construction permits filed in the week of July 17, 2026 included several significant residential projects: an 112,279-square-foot mixed-use development with 50 apartments in Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens; an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project with 86 apartments in Queens (Vernon Boulevard); and two nine-story apartment buildings with 99 units each (128,738 SF combined) in the Bronx (Tremont/East Tremont Avenue). Major alterations include Brookfield's 192,245 SF renovation at 250 Vestry (Financial District) and JPMorgan Chase's 30,000 SF renovation at 126 Myrtle Avenue in Downtown Brooklyn.
stories.impact: Ongoing multifamily permit flow across Brooklyn, Queens, and Bronx signals sustained development appetite outside Manhattan; mixed-use zoning preferences (retail+ residential) reflect evolving neighborhood demand and transit orientation. Steady permit volume confirms capital availability and leasing velocity to support new construction; monitor approval timelines and occupancy rates to assess absorption capacity. Developers should prioritize transit-proximate mixed-use projects in outer boroughs; land costs and entitlement timelines remain challenging but improving.

stories.title: Related Urban targets aging Coconut Grove housing for two-tower project
stories.excerpt: Related Urban Development Group wants to replace the majority of an aging public housing complex in Coconut Grove with high-rise apartments that contain some affordable housing under Florida’s Live Local Act. The Miami-based affordable housing subsidiary of Related Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower multifamily project with some retail on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site at 3150 and 3160 Mundy Street and 3181 Douglas Road, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The proposal seeks to demolish a 100-unit apartment building completed in 1975 and a 65-unit building completed in 1982, while preserving a 68-unit apartment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Urban (Related Group's affordable-housing subsidiary) filing Magnus Grove & Stirrup Plaza in Coconut Grove, Miami
Two-tower, 750-unit project (22-story, 25-story) replacing 165 demolished units; leveraging Florida Live Local Act
6.1-acre Miami-Dade-owned site; 40%+ units at below-market rates; retail, library, 796-space parking garage; CFE Architects
stories.summary: Related Urban Development Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower mixed-use project in Coconut Grove with 750 apartments on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site. The development will demolish two aging public-housing buildings (165 units) and replace them with a 22-story tower (348 units) and a 25-story tower (402 units), plus 7,300 square feet of retail, a library, and 796-space parking garage. At least 40 percent of units will be offered below-market rates to qualify for Florida's Live Local Act, which allows developers to bypass local zoning restrictions on height and density.
stories.impact: Live Local Act densification in Coconut Grove signals Miami-Dade's continued appetite for public-housing modernization via high-rise development; Related Urban's strategy of county partnership (site control, tax incentives, regulatory streamlining) offers replicable model for affordable-housing developers in other Florida metros. Developers should monitor Live Local Act amendments and Rapid Transit Zone sites; exemption from local review (county-level review only) accelerates entitlements. This project template (public-land + density bonus + affordable mix) will likely proliferate in Miami-Dade and other South Florida counties, reshaping supply dynamics and rents in legacy neighborhoods.

stories.title: BlackRock, MGX buy Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in $40B deal
stories.excerpt: A joint venture that includes BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX is acquiring Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in a $40 billion deal. The group is also planning to invest $5 billion in the firm it bought from Macquarie Asset Management. The capital injection is primarily to help Aligned expand capacity, according to Bloomberg. Aligned, who owns and operates data centers across the Americas, including in Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil and Mexico, has more than 6.4 GW in operational and planned output capacity, according to the outlet. Acquiring Aligned was one of BlackRock’s largest infrastructure investments, and was called […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: BlackRock Global Infrastructure Partners & Abu Dhabi's MGX acquired Aligned Data Centers for $40B; Plano-based
Partners injecting $5B into Aligned; 6.4 GW operational/planned capacity across Americas (Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil, Mexico)
Aligned operates 50 campuses, 78 data centers; deal among BlackRock's largest infrastructure investments
stories.summary: A joint venture combining BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX acquired Aligned Data Centers (Plano, Texas) in a $40 billion transaction, with a concurrent $5 billion capital injection. Aligned operates more than 6.4 gigawatts of operational and planned capacity across the Americas, including facilities in Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil, and Mexico. As of acquisition, the company operated or was constructing 50 data-center campuses with 78 facilities.
stories.impact: Massive data-center capital deployment in North Texas (Dallas core and regional footprint) signals sustained AI/generative-AI infrastructure competition; power demand, grid strain, and land utilization will reshape regional development economics. Developers should monitor ERCOT capacity constraints, utility infrastructure costs, and local opposition to data-center sprawl; these factors will constrain multifamily/mixed-use development in areas competing for power and land. However, data-center employment clusters (Plano, Richardson, Addison) will drive ancillary multifamily demand for technical workers; track workforce housing velocity in North Texas secondary markets adjacent to data-center hubs.

stories.title: London’s housing crisis collides with planning system frozen in time
stories.excerpt: London’s chronic housing shortage may have no end in sight. The United Kingdom capital is falling dramatically short of its homebuilding goals as a decades-old planning system, rising construction costs and political resistance stall development, according to the Wall Street Journal. The city targets 88,000 homes annually but started construction on just 4,170 units in the 2024-25 fiscal year, even as its population grew by roughly 100,000 people. London would need another 1.1 million homes simply to match the Western European average for housing per capita, according to the Centre for Policy Studies. The shortfall is pushing first-time home prices […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: London constructing 4,170 homes (2024-25 FY); target 88,000/year; shortfall pushes first-time home prices to ~$672K USD
Population grew ~100K annually; supply deficit: 1.1M homes needed to match Western European per-capita average
Planning bottlenecks: borough-level evaluation, subjective criteria, political opposition; example: Shoreditch Works rejected after 4 years, 9,000 pages
stories.summary: London's housing crisis is deepening as the city targets 88,000 new homes annually but started construction on just 4,170 units in fiscal year 2024-25, while population grew by roughly 100,000 people. First-time home prices have soared to approximately $672,000 USD (about 10 times average annual salary), and renters spend 42 percent of income on housing. The Centre for Policy Studies estimates London needs an additional 1.1 million homes simply to match Western European per-capita housing averages. Developers cite London's postwar planning regime—which evaluates projects individually through subjective borough-level review processes—as the primary bottleneck; projects can face years of review and rejection despite extensive documentation (e.g., Shoreditch Works spent four years and 9,000 pages before being rejected over "industrial heritage" concerns).
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on U.S. multifamily development; illustrates regulatory and political barriers to density in legacy Anglo-American markets. However, London's housing shortage (4,170 vs. 88,000 target) and reliance on subjective planning review offer cautionary lessons for U.S. developers navigating local opposition and entitlement timelines. Developers should monitor regulatory reform discussions in London and other constrained UK markets; if planning liberalization occurs, it could reshape institutional investor appetite for London development, indirectly affecting U.S. capital availability. For now, London's underinvestment in housing is a geopolitical footnote with no direct development implications for U.S. multifamily.
